point_001: The paper lacks theoretical analysis explaining why contextual temperature scaling improves convergence or leads to better loss landscapes, making it difficult to assess its value as a general training method.
point_002: The experimental baselines are weak; only MoS is used as a comparison, and stronger baselines such as Transformer or GPT-based language models are absent.
point_003: The method has not been evaluated on downstream NLU or NLG tasks, limiting evidence of its practical utility beyond perplexity on standard language modeling benchmarks.
point_004: The paper does not sufficiently differentiate its approach from prior dynamic/attended temperature scaling methods, failing to explain why parameterizing temperature with a DNN is superior to existing frameworks.
point_005: The qualitative case analysis relies on too few cherry-picked examples and needs more comprehensive illustration to be convincing.
point_006: The motivation for using a softmax (rather than, e.g., sigmoid) to compute per-token temperature scalars is not explained, especially given that softmax causes the scalars to compete with one another.
point_007: The connection to thermodynamics is not clearly established, making the use of the term 'temperature' potentially misleading in this context.
point_008: The relationship between the proposed method and the softmax bottleneck problem is not discussed; it is unclear whether element-wise scaling can effectively increase the rank of the output distribution and serve as an efficient alternative to high-rank language models.
point_009: The writing quality needs significant polishing throughout the paper to improve clarity and presentation.
